user_query:
I’m a public librarian who isn’t into motorsports, but I’ve been asked to support a small “Motorsports 101: Watching Without the Jargon” info corner. Please use the committee brief at input/committee_brief.md and do the following, producing all outputs under the outputs/ folder:

1) Use an Internet search engine to locate at least 8 freely accessible, beginner‑friendly resources that help non‑fans understand motorsports basics (e.g., plain‑language glossaries, safety explainers, and “how to watch” guides). Favor official or educational organizations over fan blogs or brand marketing. Do not include betting/gambling, brand promotional pages, or highly technical deep dives.

2) Save your initial findings to outputs/resources_raw.csv with one row per resource and these required columns: title, organization, domain, year (if available), brief_description (1–2 sentences), access_type (free/paid), audience_tag (e.g., beginner/teen/adult), notes_on_bias_or_marketing, authority_score (1–5), clarity_score (1–5), relevance_score (1–5), accessibility_score (1–5). Assign the 1–5 scores based on the definitions and priorities in input/committee_brief.md.

3) Filter, sort, and rank the records per the brief: keep only free, introductory resources; deduplicate by title; then compute weighted_score = 0.4*authority_score + 0.3*clarity_score + 0.2*relevance_score + 0.1*accessibility_score. Save the result to outputs/resources_ranked.csv, sorted by weighted_score descending. Include all columns from resources_raw.csv plus: weighted_score (numeric to one decimal place) and top5 (TRUE/FALSE).

4) Prepare meeting notes for our programming committee at outputs/meeting_notes.md. Use the meeting details and agenda from input/committee_brief.md. Include: (a) date/time and attendees; (b) agenda; (c) a ranked shortlist section listing the top 5 items from outputs/resources_ranked.csv with 1–2 sentence justifications tied to the scoring criteria; and (d) concrete action items with an owner and due date for each (e.g., permissions, printing quantities, and drafting assignments).

5) Draft a one‑page, plain‑language handout at outputs/handout_draft.md titled exactly: “Quick Guide: How to Watch a Race When You’re Not a Fan.” Include these sections in order: (1) Why people enjoy watching (3–4 short, non‑jargony bullets), (2) What to focus on during any race (3–5 bullets applicable across series), (3) Safety and etiquette (bullets emphasizing safety culture and respectful viewing), (4) Further reading. In the Further reading section, list exactly the top 3 resources by title and organization from outputs/resources_ranked.csv (no URLs). Avoid brand name promotion and specialized jargon.

All three core steps—performing live search engine queries, filtering/sorting/ranking per the brief, and preparing meeting notes with action items—are required. Keep the tone neutral and accessible throughout, as I’m not a motorsports enthusiast. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
